text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Sarah Nguyen 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Sarah Nguyen, 

Mr Daniel Harper, DOB: 22-03-1987, Hospital No: A942715 NHS No. 9812643075  
Flat 12, 4 Brook Mews, London W2 3BQ  

Diagnosis: 

Seizure disorder; current pattern as detailed below. 

Aetiology: 

No clear structural or genetic cause identified to date; previous MRI brain (2023) and routine EEG (2024) non-diagnostic. Further characterisation is ongoing. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Seasonal allergic rhinitis; intermittent lower back pain related to childcare lifting; reports fragmented sleep as primary caregiver to two young children, with frequent night-time awakenings. 

Management Plan: 

- Continue current regular medication as per GP record and prior clinic plan: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily. 
- Night-time interruptions remain an issue; we discussed sleep hygiene and practical adjustments around childcare duties. I provided a letter for his partner’s employer supporting temporary flexibility to share overnight responsibilities from a health perspective. 
- Arrange ambulatory EEG with event marker and concurrent serum lamotrigine level. 
- Safety: reinforced SUDEP risk awareness, seizure first aid, and water/heights/heat precautions; advised supervised bathing for the children and avoiding solo ladder or roof work. 
- Driving: he is aware of DVLA requirements and confirms he is not driving. 
- Prescribe midazolam oromucosal 10 mg for prolonged convulsive events >5 minutes; training refreshed. 
- Follow-up in four months, sooner if cluster events occur. 

Clinical Summary: 

He attended with his partner and their toddler; he is the primary caregiver during weekdays and reports that his night-time sleep is often interrupted by childcare needs. He describes that Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. Although this appears contradictory, on discussion he clarified that there have been no additional events since his interim GP telephone review two weeks ago, when the above overall pattern was documented. We agreed to maintain a detailed seizure and sleep diary to reconcile the timeline. 

He notes peri-sleep deprivation exacerbations, particularly after nights with multiple awakenings. There is no clear alcohol trigger; he rarely drinks. No new head injuries, febrile illnesses, or medication changes. Appetite and weight are stable. Mood is described as “stressed but coping”; we screened for anxiety and depression (brief) with borderline scores. He is interested in peer support; we provided Epilepsy Action resources and our clinic nurse contact. 

Examination today was unremarkable; no focal neurological deficits. Vitals stable. Medication adherence appears good; no rash or systemic adverse effects reported. 

Plan as above. I will write again once the ambulatory EEG and drug levels are available. Please let me know if there are any concerns, including breakthrough clusters or injuries. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: From the Clinical Summary: explicit frequencies are provided for different seizure types. Quotes: 'he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month' (1 to 2 per month), 'He has daily absences' (daily = 1 per day or more), and 'experiences focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks' (~1 per 3 to 4 weeks). Rule: choose the highest frequency across types. Daily absences represent the most frequent pattern. No statement of ≥6 months seizure freedom; the note about 'no further seizures' refers only to the short interval since a GP review two weeks ago, which does not meet the 6‑month seizure‑free criterion. Therefore, final label is 1 per day, based on 'He has daily absences.'
reference: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported
